title: Wi-Fi

infobox.value: [Personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer"), [video game consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console"), [smart devices](/wiki/Smart_device "Smart device"), [televisions](/wiki/Television "Television"), [printers](/wiki/Printer_(computing) "Printer (computing)"), [security cameras](/wiki/Security_camera "Security camera")

([/ˈwaɪfaɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"))[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[a]](#cite_note-3) is a family of [wireless network](/wiki/Wireless_network "Wireless network") protocols based on the [IEEE 802.11](/wiki/IEEE_802.11 "IEEE 802.11") family of standards, which are commonly used for [local area networking](/wiki/Wireless_LAN "Wireless LAN") of devices and [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") access, allowing nearby digital devices to exchange data by [radio waves](/wiki/Radio_wave "Radio wave"). These are the most widely used computer networks, used globally in [home and small office networks](/wiki/Small_office/home_office "Small office/home office") to link devices and to provide [Internet access](/wiki/Internet_access "Internet access") with [wireless routers](/wiki/Wireless_router "Wireless router") and [wireless access points](/wiki/Wireless_access_point "Wireless access point") in public places such as coffee shops, restaurants, hotels, libraries, and airports.

**Wi-Fi** ([/ˈwaɪfaɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"))[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[a]](#cite_note-3) is a family of [wireless network](/wiki/Wireless_network "Wireless network") protocols based on the [IEEE 802.11](/wiki/IEEE_802.11 "IEEE 802.11") family of standards, which are commonly used for [local area networking](/wiki/Wireless_LAN "Wireless LAN") of devices and [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") access, allowing nearby digital devices to exchange data by [radio waves](/wiki/Radio_wave "Radio wave"). These are the most widely used computer networks, used globally in [home and small office networks](/wiki/Small_office/home_office "Small office/home office") to link devices and to provide [Internet access](/wiki/Internet_access "Internet access") with [wireless routers](/wiki/Wireless_router "Wireless router") and [wireless access points](/wiki/Wireless_access_point "Wireless access point") in public places such as coffee shops, restaurants, hotels, libraries, and airports.

*Wi-Fi* is a trademark of the [Wi-Fi Alliance](/wiki/Wi-Fi_Alliance "Wi-Fi Alliance"), which restricts the use of the term "*Wi-Fi Certified*" to products that successfully complete [interoperability](/wiki/Interoperability_Solutions_for_European_Public_Administrations "Interoperability Solutions for European Public Administrations") certification testing.[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5)[[5]](#cite_note-6) Non-compliant hardware is simply referred to as [WLAN](/wiki/WLAN "WLAN"), and it may or may not work with "*Wi-Fi Certified*" devices. As of 2017,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi&action=edit) the Wi-Fi Alliance consisted of more than 800 companies from around the world.[[6]](#cite_note-alliance-history-7) As of 2019,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi&action=edit) over 3.05 billion Wi-Fi-enabled devices are shipped globally each year.[[7]](#cite_note-global-forecast-8)

Wi-Fi uses multiple parts of the [IEEE 802](/wiki/IEEE_802 "IEEE 802") [protocol](/wiki/Communication_protocol "Communication protocol") family and is designed to work well with its wired sibling, [Ethernet](/wiki/Ethernet "Ethernet"). Compatible devices can network through wireless access points with each other as well as with wired devices and the Internet. Different versions of Wi-Fi are specified by various [IEEE 802.11](/wiki/IEEE_802.11 "IEEE 802.11") protocol standards, with different radio technologies determining radio bands, maximum ranges, and speeds that may be achieved. Wi-Fi most commonly uses the 2.4 gigahertz (120 mm) [UHF](/wiki/UHF "UHF") and 5 gigahertz (60 mm) [SHF](/wiki/Super_high_frequency "Super high frequency") radio bands, with the 6 gigahertz SHF band used in newer generations of the standard; these bands are subdivided into multiple channels. Channels can be shared between networks, but, within range, only one transmitter can transmit on a channel at a time.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/TP-Link_AX1500_Wi-Fi_6_Router_Front.jpg/250px-TP-Link_AX1500_Wi-Fi_6_Router_Front.jpg)](/wiki/File:TP-Link_AX1500_Wi-Fi_6_Router_Front.jpg)

TP-Link AX1500 [Wi-Fi 6](/wiki/Wi-Fi_6 "Wi-Fi 6") router

Wi-Fi's radio bands work best for [line-of-sight](/wiki/Line-of-sight_propagation "Line-of-sight propagation") use. Common obstructions, such as walls, pillars, home appliances, etc., may greatly reduce range, but this also helps minimize interference between different networks in crowded environments. The range of an access point is about 20 m (66 ft) indoors, while some access points claim up to a 150 m (490 ft) range outdoors. Hotspot coverage can be as small as a single room with walls that block radio waves or as large as many square kilometers using multiple overlapping access points with [roaming](/wiki/Roaming "Roaming") permitted between them. Over time, the speed and [spectral efficiency](/wiki/Spectral_efficiency "Spectral efficiency") of Wi-Fi has increased. As of 2025,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi&action=edit) some versions of Wi-Fi, running on suitable hardware at close range, can achieve speeds of 23 Gbit/s ([gigabit](/wiki/Gigabit "Gigabit") per second).[[8]](#cite_note-9)

## History

Main article: [IEEE 802.11 § History](/wiki/IEEE_802.11#History "IEEE 802.11")

A 1985 ruling by the U.S. Federal Communications Commission released parts of the [ISM bands](/wiki/ISM_band "ISM band") for unlicensed use for communications in the United States.[[9]](#cite_note-10) These frequency bands include the same 2.4 GHz bands used by equipment such as [microwave ovens](/wiki/Microwave_oven "Microwave oven"), and are thus subject to interference.[[10]](#cite_note-scienceabc-11)

In 1991 in the city of [Nieuwegein](/wiki/Nieuwegein "Nieuwegein") in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), the [NCR Corporation](/wiki/NCR_Corporation "NCR Corporation") and [AT&T](/wiki/AT%26T_Corporation "AT&T Corporation") invented the precursor to 802.11,[[11]](#cite_note-birth-12) intended for use in cashier systems, under the name [WaveLAN](/wiki/WaveLAN "WaveLAN"). NCR's [Vic Hayes](/wiki/Vic_Hayes "Vic Hayes"), who held the chair of IEEE 802.11 for ten years, along with [Bell Labs](/wiki/Bell_Labs "Bell Labs") engineer Bruce Tuch, approached the [Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers](/wiki/Institute_of_Electrical_and_Electronics_Engineers "Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers") (IEEE) to create a standard and were involved in designing the initial 802.11b and 802.11a specifications within the IEEE.[[12]](#cite_note-13) They have both been subsequently inducted into the Wi-Fi NOW Hall of Fame.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

In 1989 in Australia, a team of scientists began working on wireless LAN technology.[[14]](#cite_note-15) A prototype [test bed](/wiki/Test_bed "Test bed") for a [wireless local area network](/wiki/Wireless_local_area_network "Wireless local area network") (WLAN) was developed in 1992 by a team of researchers from the Radiophysics Division of the [CSIRO](/wiki/CSIRO "CSIRO") (Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organisation) in Australia, led by [John O'Sullivan](/wiki/John_O%27Sullivan_(engineer) "John O'Sullivan (engineer)").[[15]](#cite_note-csirowifi-16) A patent for Wi Fi was lodged by the CSIRO in 1992.[[16]](#cite_note-O’Sullivan_147–147-17)

The first version of the 802.11 protocol was released in 1997, and provided up to 2 Mbit/s link speeds. This was updated in 1999 with [802.11b](/wiki/802.11b "802.11b") to permit 11 Mbit/s link speeds.

In 1999, the [Wi-Fi Alliance](/wiki/Wi-Fi_Alliance "Wi-Fi Alliance") formed as a trade association to hold the Wi-Fi trademark under which most IEEE 802.11 products are sold.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Apple_Airport_Extreme_802.11g_card.jpg/250px-Apple_Airport_Extreme_802.11g_card.jpg)](/wiki/File:Apple_Airport_Extreme_802.11g_card.jpg)

Apple AirPort Extreme installed in an iBook G4

The major commercial breakthrough came with [Apple Inc.](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") adopting Wi-Fi for their [iBook](/wiki/IBook "IBook") series of laptops in 1999.[[11]](#cite_note-birth-12) It was the first mass consumer product to offer Wi-Fi network connectivity, which was then branded by Apple as [AirPort](/wiki/AirPort "AirPort").[[18]](#cite_note-19) This was in collaboration with the same group that helped create the standard: [Vic Hayes](/wiki/Vic_Hayes "Vic Hayes"), Bruce Tuch, [Cees Links](/wiki/Cees_Links "Cees Links"), Rich McGinn, and others from [Lucent](/wiki/Lucent "Lucent").[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21)

In 2000, Radiata, a group of Australian scientists connected to the CSIRO, were the first to use the 802.11a standard on chips connected to a Wi-Fi network.[[16]](#cite_note-O’Sullivan_147–147-17)

Wi-Fi uses a large number of [patents](/wiki/Patent "Patent") held by multiple different organizations.[[21]](#cite_note-22) Australia,[[22]](#cite_note-23) the United States[[23]](#cite_note-24) and the Netherlands[[24]](#cite_note-25) simultaneously claim the invention of Wi-Fi, and a consensus has not been reached globally.[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-patent-troll-27) In 2009, the Australian [CSIRO](/wiki/CSIRO "CSIRO") was awarded $200 million after a patent settlement with 14 technology companies, with a further $220 million awarded in 2012 after legal proceedings with 23 companies.[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30)

In 2016, the CSIRO's WLAN prototype test bed was chosen as Australia's contribution to the exhibition *[A History of the World in 100 Objects](/wiki/A_History_of_the_World_in_100_Objects "A History of the World in 100 Objects")* held in the [National Museum of Australia](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Australia "National Museum of Australia").[[15]](#cite_note-csirowifi-16)

## Etymology and terminology

The name *Wi-Fi*, commercially used as early as August 1999,[[30]](#cite_note-31) was coined by the brand-consulting firm Interbrand. The Wi-Fi Alliance had hired Interbrand to create a name that was "a little catchier than 'IEEE 802.11b Direct Sequence'."[[31]](#cite_note-boing-32)[[32]](#cite_note-wifi_debunked-33) According to Phil Belanger, a founding member of the Wi-Fi Alliance, the term *Wi-Fi* was chosen from a list of ten names that Interbrand proposed.[[31]](#cite_note-boing-32) Interbrand also created the Wi-Fi [logo](/wiki/Logo "Logo"). The [yin-yang](/wiki/Yin-yang "Yin-yang") Wi-Fi logo indicates the certification of a product for [interoperability](/wiki/Interoperability "Interoperability").[[33]](#cite_note-wifi_securing_2003-34) The name is often written as *WiFi*, *Wifi*, or *wifi*, but these are not approved by the Wi-Fi Alliance.

The name *Wi-Fi* is *not* short-form for 'Wireless Fidelity',[[34]](#cite_note-35) although the Wi-Fi Alliance did use the [advertising slogan](/wiki/Advertising_slogan "Advertising slogan") "The Standard for Wireless Fidelity" for a short time after the brand name was created,[[31]](#cite_note-boing-32)[[33]](#cite_note-wifi_securing_2003-34)[[35]](#cite_note-wifi_deployment_2004-36) and the Wi-Fi Alliance was also called the "Wireless Fidelity Alliance Inc." in some publications.[[36]](#cite_note-37) [IEEE](/wiki/IEEE "IEEE"), a separate but related organization, has stated "WiFi is a short name for Wireless Fidelity" on their website.[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39) The name *Wi-Fi* was partly chosen because it sounds similar to [Hi-Fi](/wiki/Hi-Fi "Hi-Fi"), which consumers take to mean *high fidelity* or *high quality*. Interbrand hoped consumers would find the name catchy, and that they would assume this *wireless* protocol has high fidelity because of its name.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

Other technologies intended for fixed points, including [Motorola Canopy](/wiki/Motorola_Canopy "Motorola Canopy"), are usually called *[fixed wireless](/wiki/Fixed_wireless "Fixed wireless")*. Alternative wireless technologies include [Zigbee](/wiki/Zigbee "Zigbee"), [Z-Wave](/wiki/Z-Wave "Z-Wave"), [Bluetooth](/wiki/Bluetooth "Bluetooth") and [mobile phone standards](/wiki/Mobile_phone_standards "Mobile phone standards").

To connect to a Wi-Fi LAN, a computer must be equipped with a [wireless network interface controller](/wiki/Wireless_network_interface_controller "Wireless network interface controller"). The combination of a computer and an interface controller is called a *[station](/wiki/Station_(networking) "Station (networking)")*. Stations are identified by one or more [MAC addresses](/wiki/MAC_address "MAC address").

Wi-Fi nodes often operate in infrastructure mode in which all communications go through a base station. *[Ad hoc mode](/wiki/Ad_hoc_mode "Ad hoc mode")* refers to devices communicating directly with each other, without communicating with an access point.

A [service set](/wiki/Service_set_(802.11_network) "Service set (802.11 network)") is the set of all the devices associated with a particular Wi-Fi network. Devices in a service set need not be on the same wavebands or channels. A service set can be local, independent, extended, mesh, or a combination. Each service set has an associated identifier, a 32-byte service set identifier (SSID), which identifies the network. The [SSID](/wiki/SSID "SSID") is configured within the devices that are part of the network. A basic service set (BSS) is a group of stations that share the same wireless channel, SSID, and other settings that have wirelessly connected, usually to the same access point.[[40]](#cite_note-IEEE802.11-2007-41): 3.6 Each BSS is identified by a MAC address called the *BSSID*.

## Certification

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Wifi_certified_logo.png/250px-Wifi_certified_logo.png)](/wiki/File:Wifi_certified_logo.png)

Wi-Fi certification logo

The [IEEE](/wiki/IEEE "IEEE") does not test equipment for compliance with their standards. The [Wi-Fi Alliance](/wiki/Wi-Fi_Alliance "Wi-Fi Alliance") was formed in 1999 to establish and enforce standards for interoperability and [backward compatibility](/wiki/Backward_compatibility "Backward compatibility"), and to promote [wireless](/wiki/Wireless "Wireless") local-area-network technology. The Wi-Fi Alliance enforces the use of the Wi-Fi brand to technologies based on the [IEEE 802.11](/wiki/IEEE_802.11 "IEEE 802.11") standards from the IEEE. Manufacturers with membership in the Wi-Fi Alliance, whose products pass the certification process, gain the right to mark those products with the Wi-Fi logo. Specifically, the certification process requires conformance to the IEEE 802.11 radio standards, the [WPA and WPA2](/wiki/Wi-Fi_Protected_Access "Wi-Fi Protected Access") security standards, and the [EAP](/wiki/Extensible_Authentication_Protocol "Extensible Authentication Protocol") authentication standard. Certification may optionally include tests of IEEE 802.11 draft standards, interaction with cellular-phone technology in converged devices, and features relating to security set-up, multimedia, and power-saving.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

Not every Wi-Fi device is submitted for certification. The lack of Wi-Fi certification does not necessarily imply that a device is incompatible with other Wi-Fi devices.[[42]](#cite_note-43) The Wi-Fi Alliance may or may not sanction derivative terms, such as [Super Wi-Fi](/wiki/Super_Wi-Fi "Super Wi-Fi"),[[43]](#cite_note-44) coined by the US [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") (FCC) to describe proposed networking in the UHF TV band in the US.[[44]](#cite_note-Super-45)

Equipment frequently supports multiple versions of Wi-Fi. To communicate, devices must use a common Wi-Fi version. The versions differ between the radio wavebands they operate on, the radio bandwidth they occupy, the maximum data rates they can support and other details. Some versions permit the use of multiple antennas, which permits greater speeds as well as reduced interference.

Historically, the equipment listed the versions of Wi-Fi supported using the name of the IEEE standards. In 2018, the [Wi-Fi Alliance](/wiki/Wi-Fi_Alliance "Wi-Fi Alliance") introduced simplified Wi-Fi generational numbering to indicate equipment that supports Wi-Fi 4 ([802.11n](/wiki/802.11n "802.11n")), Wi-Fi 5 ([802.11ac](/wiki/802.11ac "802.11ac")) and Wi-Fi 6 ([802.11ax](/wiki/802.11ax "802.11ax")). These generations have a high degree of backward compatibility with previous versions. The alliance has stated that the generational level 4, 5, or 6 can be indicated in the user interface when connected, along with the signal strength.[[49]](#cite_note-51)[[50]](#cite_note-generational-52)

The most important standards affecting Wi‑Fi are: 802.11a, 802.11b, 802.11g, 802.11n (**Wi-Fi 4**), 802.11h, 802.11i, 802.11-2007, 802.11–2012, 802.11ac (**Wi-Fi 5**),[[50]](#cite_note-generational-52) 802.11ad, 802.11af, 802.11-2016, 802.11ah, 802.11ai, 802.11aj, [802.11aq](/wiki/802.11aq "802.11aq"), 802.11ax (**Wi-Fi 6**),[[50]](#cite_note-generational-52) [802.11ay](/wiki/802.11ay "802.11ay").

## Uses

### Internet

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/SSID_ESS.svg/250px-SSID_ESS.svg.png)](/wiki/File:SSID_ESS.svg)

An example of a service set called *WiFi Wikipedia* consisting of two basic service sets. The clients automatically [roam](/wiki/Roaming "Roaming") between the two BSSs without the user having to explicitly connect to the second network.

Wi-Fi technology may be used to provide local network and [Internet access](/wiki/Internet_access "Internet access") to devices that are within Wi-Fi range of one or more routers that are connected to the Internet. The coverage of one or more interconnected access points can extend from an area as small as a few rooms to as large as many square kilometres. Coverage in the larger area may require a group of access points with overlapping coverage. For example, public outdoor Wi-Fi technology has been used successfully in [wireless mesh networks](/wiki/Wireless_mesh_network "Wireless mesh network") in London. An international example is [Fon](/wiki/Fon_Wireless "Fon Wireless").

Wi-Fi provides services in private homes, businesses, as well as in public spaces. [Wi-Fi hotspots](/wiki/Wi-Fi_hotspot "Wi-Fi hotspot") may be set up either free of charge or commercially, often using a [captive portal](/wiki/Captive_portal "Captive portal") webpage for access. Organizations, enthusiasts, authorities and [businesses](/wiki/Business "Business"), such as airports, hotels, and restaurants, often provide free or paid-use hotspots to attract customers, to provide services to promote business in selected areas. [Routers](/wiki/Router_(computing) "Router (computing)") often incorporate a [digital subscriber line](/wiki/Digital_subscriber_line "Digital subscriber line") modem or a [cable modem](/wiki/Cable_modem "Cable modem") and a Wi-Fi access point, are frequently set up in homes and other buildings, to provide Internet access for the structure.

Similarly, battery-powered routers may include a [mobile broadband modem](/wiki/Mobile_broadband_modem "Mobile broadband modem") and a Wi-Fi access point. When subscribed to a cellular data carrier, they allow nearby Wi-Fi stations to access the Internet. A number of smartphones have a built-in [mobile hotspot](/wiki/Mobile_hotspot "Mobile hotspot") capability of this sort, though carriers often disable the feature, or charge a separate fee to enable it. Standalone devices such as [MiFi](/wiki/MiFi "MiFi")- and [WiBro](/wiki/WiBro "WiBro")-branded devices provide the capability. Some laptops that have a cellular modem card can also act as mobile Internet Wi-Fi access points.

Multiple traditional university campuses in the developed world provide at least partial Wi-Fi coverage. [Carnegie Mellon University](/wiki/Carnegie_Mellon_University "Carnegie Mellon University") built the first campus-wide wireless Internet network, called [Wireless Andrew](/wiki/Wireless_Andrew "Wireless Andrew"), at its [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh") campus in 1993 before Wi-Fi branding existed.[[51]](#cite_note-53)[[52]](#cite_note-54)[[53]](#cite_note-55) A number of universities collaborate in providing Wi-Fi access to students and staff through the [Eduroam](/wiki/Eduroam "Eduroam") international authentication infrastructure.

### City-wide

Further information: [Municipal wireless network](/wiki/Municipal_wireless_network "Municipal wireless network")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Metro_Wireless_Node.jpg/250px-Metro_Wireless_Node.jpg)](/wiki/File:Metro_Wireless_Node.jpg)

An outdoor Wi-Fi access point

In the early 2000s, multiple cities around the world announced plans to construct citywide Wi-Fi networks. There are a number of successful examples; in 2004, [Mysore](/wiki/Mysore "Mysore") (Mysuru) became India's first Wi-Fi-enabled city. A company called WiFiyNet has set up hotspots in Mysore, covering the whole city and a few nearby villages.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

In 2005, [St. Cloud, Florida](/wiki/St._Cloud,_Florida "St. Cloud, Florida") and [Sunnyvale, California](/wiki/Sunnyvale,_California "Sunnyvale, California"), became the first cities in the United States to offer citywide free Wi-Fi (from [MetroFi](/wiki/MetroFi "MetroFi")).[[55]](#cite_note-57) [Minneapolis](/wiki/Minneapolis "Minneapolis") has generated $1.2 million in profit annually for [its provider](/wiki/Minneapolis_wireless_internet_network "Minneapolis wireless internet network").[[56]](#cite_note-58)

In May 2010, the then [London](/wiki/London "London") mayor [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") pledged to have London-wide Wi-Fi by 2012.[[57]](#cite_note-59) Several [boroughs](/wiki/London_boroughs "London boroughs") including [Westminster](/wiki/City_of_Westminster "City of Westminster") and [Islington](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Islington "London Borough of Islington")[[58]](#cite_note-60)[[59]](#cite_note-61) already had extensive outdoor Wi-Fi coverage at that point.

[New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") announced a city-wide campaign to convert old [phone booths](/wiki/Phone_booths "Phone booths") into [digital kiosks](/wiki/Digital_kiosk "Digital kiosk") in 2014. The project, titled [LinkNYC](/wiki/LinkNYC "LinkNYC"), has created a network of kiosks that serve as public Wi-Fi hotspots, high-definition screens and [landlines](/wiki/Landline "Landline"). Installation of the screens began in late 2015. The city government plans to implement more than seven thousand kiosks over time, eventually making LinkNYC the largest and fastest public, government-operated Wi-Fi network in the world.[[60]](#cite_note-Forbes-PayPhonesWifiKiosks-2014-62)[[61]](#cite_note-WNYC-GoodbyePayPhones-20162-63)[[62]](#cite_note-WashingtonPost-PayphoneOfTheFuture-2014-64)[[63]](#cite_note-NYCGov-MayorAnnouncesWinner-20142-65)[[64]](#cite_note-NYDailyNews-PayphonesReplaced-20162-66) The [UK](/wiki/UK "UK") has planned a similar project across major cities of the country, with the project's first implementation in the [London Borough of Camden](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Camden "London Borough of Camden").[[65]](#cite_note-67)

Officials in South Korea's capital [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul") were moving to provide free Internet access at more than 10,000 locations around the city, including outdoor public spaces, major streets, and densely populated residential areas. Seoul was planning to grant leases to KT, [LG](/wiki/LG "LG") Telecom, and SK Telecom. The companies were supposed to invest $44 million in the project, which was to be completed in 2015.[[66]](#cite_note-Voice_of_America-68)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

### Geolocation

[Wi-Fi positioning systems](/wiki/Wi-Fi_positioning_system "Wi-Fi positioning system") use known positions of Wi-Fi hotspots to identify a device's location.[[67]](#cite_note-KolodziejHjelm2017-69)[[68]](#cite_note-70)[[69]](#cite_note-71) It is used when [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS") isn't suitable due to issues like signal interference or slow satellite acquisition.[[70]](#cite_note-72) This includes assisted GPS, urban hotspot databases, and indoor positioning systems.[[71]](#cite_note-73) Wi-Fi positioning relies on measuring signal strength ([RSSI](/wiki/RSSI "RSSI")) and fingerprinting.[[72]](#cite_note-:2-74)[[73]](#cite_note-75)[[74]](#cite_note-:7-76)[[75]](#cite_note-:8-77) Parameters like [SSID](/wiki/SSID "SSID") and MAC address are crucial for identifying access points. The accuracy depends on nearby access points in the database. Signal fluctuations can cause errors, which can be reduced with noise-filtering techniques. For low precision, integrating Wi-Fi data with geographical and time information has been proposed.[[76]](#cite_note-78)[[77]](#cite_note-:1-79)

The [Wi-Fi RTT](/wiki/Wi-Fi_RTT "Wi-Fi RTT") capability introduced in [IEEE 802.11mc](/wiki/IEEE_802.11mc "IEEE 802.11mc") allows for positioning based on round trip time measurement, an improvement over the RSSI method.[[78]](#cite_note-80) The [IEEE 802.11az](/w/index.php?title=IEEE_802.11az&action=edit&redlink=1 "IEEE 802.11az (page does not exist)") standard promises further improvements in geolocation accuracy.[[79]](#cite_note-81)[[80]](#cite_note-82)

### Motion detection

[Wi-Fi sensing](/wiki/Wi-Fi_sensing "Wi-Fi sensing") is used in applications such as [motion detection](/wiki/Motion_detection "Motion detection") and [gesture recognition](/wiki/Gesture_recognition "Gesture recognition").[[81]](#cite_note-83)

## Operational principles

Wi-Fi stations communicate by sending each other [data packets](/wiki/Data_packet "Data packet"), blocks of data individually sent and delivered over radio on various channels. As with all radio, this is done by the [modulation and demodulation](/wiki/Modem "Modem") of [carrier waves](/wiki/Carrier_wave "Carrier wave"). Different versions of Wi-Fi use different techniques, 802.11b uses [direct-sequence spread spectrum](/wiki/Direct-sequence_spread_spectrum "Direct-sequence spread spectrum") on a single carrier, whereas 802.11a, Wi-Fi 4, 5 and 6 use [orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing](/wiki/Orthogonal_frequency-division_multiplexing "Orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing").[[82]](#cite_note-84)[[83]](#cite_note-oreilly-802.11ac-85)

Channels are used [half duplex](/wiki/Half_duplex "Half duplex")[[84]](#cite_note-86)[[85]](#cite_note-87) and can be [time-shared](/wiki/Time-division_multiple_access "Time-division multiple access") by multiple networks. Any packet sent by one computer is locally received by stations tuned to that channel, even if that information is intended for just one destination.[[c]](#cite_note-88) Stations typically ignore information not addressed to them.[[d]](#cite_note-promiscuous-89) The use of the same channel also means that the data bandwidth is shared, so for example, available throughput to each device is halved when two stations are actively transmitting.

As with other IEEE 802 LANs, stations come programmed with a globally unique 48-bit MAC address.[[e]](#cite_note-90) The MAC addresses are used to specify both the destination and the source of each data packet. On the reception of a transmission, the receiver uses the destination address to determine whether the transmission is relevant to the station or should be ignored.

A scheme known as [carrier-sense multiple access with collision avoidance](/wiki/Carrier-sense_multiple_access_with_collision_avoidance "Carrier-sense multiple access with collision avoidance") (CSMA/CA) governs the way stations share channels. With CSMA/CA stations attempt to avoid collisions by beginning transmission only after the channel is sensed to be idle,[[86]](#cite_note-federalstandard-91)[[87]](#cite_note-atis-92) but then transmit their packet data in its entirety. CSMA/CA cannot completely prevent collisions, as two stations may sense the channel to be idle at the same time and thus begin transmission simultaneously. A collision happens when a station receives signals from multiple stations on a channel at the same time. This corrupts the transmitted data and can require stations to re-transmit. The lost data and re-transmission reduces throughput, in some cases severely.

### Waveband

Main article: [List of WLAN channels](/wiki/List_of_WLAN_channels "List of WLAN channels")

The 802.11 standard provides several distinct [radio frequency](/wiki/Radio_frequency "Radio frequency") ranges for use in Wi-Fi communications: 900 [MHz](/wiki/MHz "MHz"), 2.4 GHz, 3.6 GHz, 4.9 GHz, 5 GHz, 6 GHz and 60 GHz [bands](/wiki/Band_(radio) "Band (radio)").[[88]](#cite_note-93)[[89]](#cite_note-94)[[90]](#cite_note-95) Each range is divided into a multitude of [channels](/wiki/Channel_(communications) "Channel (communications)"). In the standards, channels are numbered at 5 MHz spacing within a band (except in the 60 GHz band, where they are 2.16 GHz apart), and the number refers to the centre frequency of the channel. Although channels are numbered at 5 MHz spacing, transmitters generally occupy at least 20 MHz, and standards allow for neighbouring channels to be bonded together to form a wider channel for higher throughput.

Countries apply their own regulations to the allowable channels, allowed users and maximum power levels within these frequency ranges. 802.11b/g/n can use the 2.4 GHz band, operating in the United States under FCC [Part 15](/wiki/Part_15 "Part 15") rules and regulations. In this frequency band, equipment may occasionally suffer [interference](/wiki/Interference_(communication) "Interference (communication)") from microwave ovens,[[10]](#cite_note-scienceabc-11) [cordless telephones](/wiki/Cordless_telephone "Cordless telephone"), [USB 3.0](/wiki/USB_3.0 "USB 3.0") hubs,[[91]](#cite_note-96) [Bluetooth](/wiki/Bluetooth "Bluetooth") and other devices.[[92]](#cite_note-wired-97)

Spectrum assignments and operational limitations are not consistent worldwide: Australia and Europe allow for an additional two channels (12, 13) beyond the 11 permitted in the United States for the 2.4 GHz band, while Japan has three more (12–14).

802.11a/h/j/n/ac/ax can use the [5 GHz U-NII band](/wiki/U-NII "U-NII"), which, for much of the world, offers at least 23 non-overlapping 20 MHz channels. This is in contrast to the 2.4 GHz frequency band where the channels are only 5 MHz wide. In general, lower frequencies have longer range but have less capacity. The 5 GHz bands are absorbed to a greater degree by common building materials than the 2.4 GHz bands and usually give a shorter range.

As 802.11 specifications evolved to support higher throughput, the protocols have become much more efficient in their bandwidth use. Additionally, they have gained the ability to [aggregate](/wiki/Link_aggregation "Link aggregation") channels together to gain still more throughput where the bandwidth for additional channels is available. 802.11n allows for double radio spectrum bandwidth (40 MHz) per channel compared to [802.11a](/wiki/802.11a "802.11a") or 802.11g (20 MHz). 802.11n can be set to limit itself to 20 MHz bandwidth to prevent interference in dense communities.[[93]](#cite_note-98) In the 5 GHz band, 20 MHz, 40 MHz, 80 MHz, and 160 MHz channels are permitted with some restrictions, giving much faster connections.

### Communication stack

Main articles: [IEEE 802](/wiki/IEEE_802 "IEEE 802") and [IEEE 802.11](/wiki/IEEE_802.11 "IEEE 802.11")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/802.11_MAC_Frame.svg/960px-802.11_MAC_Frame.svg.png)](/wiki/File:802.11_MAC_Frame.svg "Generic 802.11 Frame")

Generic 802.11 Frame

Wi-Fi is part of the IEEE 802 protocol family. The data is organized into [802.11 frames](/wiki/802.11_frame_types "802.11 frame types") that are very similar to [Ethernet frames](/wiki/Ethernet_frame "Ethernet frame") at the data link layer, but with extra address fields. MAC addresses are used as [network addresses](/wiki/Network_address "Network address") for routing over the LAN.[[94]](#cite_note-IEEE_802.3_Clause_3.1.1-99)

Wi-Fi's MAC and [physical layer](/wiki/Physical_layer "Physical layer") (PHY) specifications are defined by IEEE 802.11 for modulating and receiving one or more carrier waves to transmit the data in the infrared, and 2.4, [3.6](/wiki/IEEE_802.11y-2008 "IEEE 802.11y-2008"), 5, 6, or [60 GHz](/wiki/IEEE_802.11ad "IEEE 802.11ad") frequency bands. They are created and maintained by the IEEE LAN/MAN Standards Committee ([IEEE 802](/wiki/IEEE_802 "IEEE 802")). The base version of the standard was released in 1997 and has had many subsequent amendments. The standard and amendments provide the basis for wireless network products using the Wi-Fi brand. While each amendment is officially revoked when incorporated in the latest version of the standard, the corporate world tends to market to the revisions because they concisely denote capabilities of their products.[[95]](#cite_note-100) As a result, in the market place, each revision tends to become its own standard.

In addition to 802.11, the IEEE 802 protocol family has specific provisions for Wi-Fi. These are required because Ethernet's cable-based media are not usually shared, whereas with wireless all transmissions are received by all stations within the range that employ that radio channel. While Ethernet has essentially negligible error rates, wireless communication media are subject to significant interference. Therefore, the accurate transmission is not guaranteed so delivery is, therefore, a [best-effort delivery](/wiki/Best-effort_delivery "Best-effort delivery") mechanism. Because of this, for Wi-Fi, the [Logical Link Control](/wiki/Logical_Link_Control "Logical Link Control") (LLC) specified by [IEEE 802.2](/wiki/IEEE_802.2 "IEEE 802.2") employs Wi-Fi's [media access control](/wiki/Media_access_control "Media access control") (MAC) protocols to manage retries without relying on higher levels of the protocol stack.[[96]](#cite_note-101)

For internetworking purposes, Wi-Fi is usually [layered](/wiki/Protocol_layering "Protocol layering") as a [link layer](/wiki/Link_layer "Link layer")[[f]](#cite_note-102) below the [internet layer](/wiki/Internet_layer "Internet layer") of the [Internet Protocol](/wiki/Internet_Protocol "Internet Protocol"). This means that nodes have an associated [internet address](/wiki/Internet_address "Internet address") and, with suitable connectivity, this allows full Internet access.

### Modes

#### Infrastructure

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Wi-Fi.gif/250px-Wi-Fi.gif)](/wiki/File:Wi-Fi.gif)

A Wi-Fi network in infrastructure mode. The print job is sent from the computer via the AP to the printer.

In infrastructure mode, which is the most common mode used, all communications go through a base station. For communications within the network, this introduces an extra use of the airwaves but has the advantage that any two stations that can communicate with the base station can also communicate through the base station, which limits issues associated with the [hidden node problem](/wiki/Hidden_node_problem "Hidden node problem") and simplifies the protocols.

#### Ad hoc and Wi-Fi direct

Wi-Fi also allows communications directly from one computer to another without an access point intermediary. This is called [*ad hoc* Wi-Fi transmission](/wiki/Wireless_ad_hoc_network "Wireless ad hoc network"). Different types of ad hoc networks exist. In the simplest case, network nodes must talk directly to each other. In more complex protocols nodes may forward packets, and nodes keep track of how to reach other nodes, even if they move around.

Ad hoc mode was first described by [Chai Keong Toh](/wiki/Chai_Keong_Toh "Chai Keong Toh") in his 1996 patent of wireless ad hoc routing,[[97]](#cite_note-103) implemented on Lucent WaveLAN 802.11a wireless on IBM [ThinkPads](/wiki/ThinkPad "ThinkPad") over a size nodes scenario spanning a region of over a mile. The success was recorded in *Mobile Computing* magazine (1999)[[98]](#cite_note-104) and later published formally in *[IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications](/wiki/IEEE_Transactions_on_Wireless_Communications "IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications")*, 2002[[99]](#cite_note-105) and *ACM SIGMETRICS Performance Evaluation Review*, 2001.[[100]](#cite_note-106)

This wireless ad hoc network mode has proven popular with [multiplayer video games](/wiki/Multiplayer_video_game "Multiplayer video game") on [handheld game consoles](/wiki/Handheld_game_console "Handheld game console"), such as the [Nintendo DS](/wiki/Nintendo_DS "Nintendo DS") and [PlayStation Portable](/wiki/PlayStation_Portable "PlayStation Portable"). It is also popular on [digital cameras](/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera"), and other [consumer electronics devices](/wiki/Consumer_electronics_devices "Consumer electronics devices"). Some devices can also share their Internet connection using ad hoc, becoming hotspots or [virtual routers](/wiki/Virtual_router "Virtual router").[[101]](#cite_note-107)

Similarly, the Wi-Fi Alliance promotes the specification [Wi-Fi Direct](/wiki/Wi-Fi_Direct "Wi-Fi Direct") for file transfers and media sharing through a new discovery and security methodology.[[102]](#cite_note-108) Wi-Fi Direct launched in October 2010.[[103]](#cite_note-109)

Another mode of direct communication over Wi-Fi is [Tunneled Direct Link Setup](/wiki/Tunneled_Direct_Link_Setup "Tunneled Direct Link Setup") (TDLS), which enables two devices on the same Wi-Fi network to communicate directly, instead of via the access point.[[104]](#cite_note-110)

### Multiple access points

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/802.11_Beacon_frame.gif/250px-802.11_Beacon_frame.gif)](/wiki/File:802.11_Beacon_frame.gif)

Access points send out [beacon frames](/wiki/Beacon_frame "Beacon frame") to announce the presence of networks.

An [Extended Service Set](/wiki/Extended_Service_Set "Extended Service Set") may be formed by deploying multiple access points that are configured with the same SSID and security settings. Wi-Fi client devices typically connect to the access point that can provide the strongest signal within that service set.[[105]](#cite_note-111)

Increasing the number of Wi-Fi access points for a network provides [redundancy](/wiki/Redundancy_(engineering) "Redundancy (engineering)"), better range, support for fast [handover](/wiki/Handover "Handover"), and increased overall network capacity by using more channels or by defining smaller [cells](/wiki/Cellular_network "Cellular network"). Except for the smallest implementations (such as home or small office networks), Wi-Fi implementations have moved toward *thin* access points, with more of the [network intelligence](/wiki/Network_intelligence "Network intelligence") housed in a centralized network appliance, relegating individual access points to the role of dumb transceivers. Outdoor applications may use [mesh](/wiki/Mesh_networking "Mesh networking") topologies.[[106]](#cite_note-112)

## Performance

See also: [Long-range Wi-Fi](/wiki/Long-range_Wi-Fi "Long-range Wi-Fi")

Wi-Fi operational range depends on factors such as the frequency band, modulation technique, [transmitter power output](/wiki/Transmitter_power_output "Transmitter power output"), receiver sensitivity, antenna gain and type, and propagation and interference characteristics in the environment. At longer distances, speed is typically reduced.

### Transmitter power

Compared to cell phones and similar technology, Wi-Fi transmitters are low-power devices. In general, the maximum amount of power that a Wi-Fi device can transmit is limited by local regulations, such as [FCC Part 15](/wiki/FCC_Part_15 "FCC Part 15") in the US. [Equivalent isotropically radiated power](/wiki/Equivalent_isotropically_radiated_power "Equivalent isotropically radiated power") (EIRP) in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") is limited to 20 [dBm](/wiki/DBm "DBm") (100 mW).

Wi-Fi, however, has higher power compared to some other standards designed to support [wireless personal area network](/wiki/Wireless_personal_area_network "Wireless personal area network") applications. For example, Bluetooth provides a much shorter [propagation](/wiki/Radio_propagation "Radio propagation") range between 1 and 100 metres (1 and 100 yards)[[107]](#cite_note-113) and so in general has a lower power consumption. Other low-power technologies such as [Zigbee](/wiki/Zigbee "Zigbee") have fairly long range, but much lower data rate. The high power consumption of Wi-Fi makes battery life in some mobile devices a concern.

### Antenna

On wireless routers with detachable antennas, it is possible to improve range by fitting upgraded antennas. An access point compliant with either [802.11b](/wiki/IEEE_802.11#802.11b "IEEE 802.11") or 802.11g, using the stock [omnidirectional antenna](/wiki/Omnidirectional_antenna "Omnidirectional antenna") might have a range of 0.1 km. The same radio with an external semi-parabolic antenna (15 dB [gain](/wiki/Gain_(antenna) "Gain (antenna)")) with a similarly equipped receiver at the far end might have a range of over 32 km.

Higher gain rating ([dBi](/wiki/DBi "DBi")) indicates deviation from a theoretical, perfect [isotropic radiator](/wiki/Isotropic_radiator "Isotropic radiator") toward a [directional antenna](/wiki/Directional_antenna "Directional antenna"), and therefore the antenna can project or accept a usable signal further in particular directions, as compared to a similar output power on a more isotropic antenna.[[108]](#cite_note-114) For example, an 8 dBi antenna used with a 100 mW driver has a similar horizontal range to a 6 dBi antenna being driven at 500 mW. This assumes that radiation in the vertical direction is not useful for communications.

### MIMO (multiple-input and multiple-output)

Main article: [MIMO](/wiki/MIMO "MIMO")

Wi-Fi 4 and higher standards allow devices to have multiple antennas on transmitters and receivers. Multiple antennas enable the equipment to exploit [multipath propagation](/wiki/Multipath_propagation "Multipath propagation") on the same frequency bands, giving higher speeds and more than doubled range.[[109]](#cite_note-wifiplanet-115)

The Wi-Fi 5 standard uses the 5 GHz band exclusively, and is capable of multi-station WLAN throughput of at least 1 gigabit per second, and a single station throughput of at least 500 Mbit/s. This standard uses several signal processing techniques such as multi-user MIMO and 4 × 4 spatial multiplexing streams, and wide channel bandwidth (160 MHz) to achieve its gigabit throughput.

### Radio propagation

With Wi-Fi signals, [line-of-sight](/wiki/Line-of-sight_propagation "Line-of-sight propagation") usually works best, but signals can absorb, reflect, [refract](/wiki/Refract "Refract"), [diffract](/wiki/Diffract "Diffract") and [fade](/wiki/Fading "Fading") through and around structures, both man-made and natural. Wi-Fi signals are very strongly affected by metallic structures (including [rebar](/wiki/Rebar "Rebar") in concrete, [low-e coatings](/wiki/Window_insulation#Low-e_coatings "Window insulation") in glazing), rock structures (including [marble](/wiki/Marble "Marble")) and water (such as found in vegetation).

Due to the complex nature of radio propagation at typical Wi-Fi frequencies, particularly around trees and buildings, algorithms can only approximately predict Wi-Fi signal strength for any given area in relation to a transmitter.[[110]](#cite_note-116) Performance associated with [long-range Wi-Fi](/wiki/Long-range_Wi-Fi "Long-range Wi-Fi") is more easily predicted, since longer links typically operate line-of-sight from towers that transmit above the surrounding foliage.

#### Distance records

Distance records (using non-standard devices) include 382 km (237 mi) in June 2007, held by Ermanno Pietrosemoli and EsLaRed of Venezuela, transferring about 3 MB/sec of data between the mountain-tops of [El Águila](/wiki/Pico_El_%C3%81guila "Pico El Águila") and Platillon.[[111]](#cite_note-117) The [Swedish National Space Agency](/wiki/Swedish_National_Space_Agency "Swedish National Space Agency") transferred data 420 km (260 mi), using 6 watt amplifiers to reach an overhead [stratospheric balloon](/wiki/Stratospheric_balloon "Stratospheric balloon").[[112]](#cite_note-118)

### Interference

Further information: [Electromagnetic interference at 2.4 GHz § Wi-Fi](/wiki/Electromagnetic_interference_at_2.4_GHz#Wi-Fi "Electromagnetic interference at 2.4 GHz")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Wi-Fi_Allocations_4.gif/250px-Wi-Fi_Allocations_4.gif)](/wiki/File:Wi-Fi_Allocations_4.gif)

Network planning frequency allocations for North America and Europe. Using these types of frequency allocations can help minimize co-channel and adjacent-channel interference.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Co-Channel_Wi-Fi_Interference_01.png/250px-Co-Channel_Wi-Fi_Interference_01.png)](/wiki/File:Co-Channel_Wi-Fi_Interference_01.png)

In the 2.4 GHz wavebands as well as others, transmitters straddle multiple channels. Overlapping channels can suffer from interference unless this is a small portion of the total received power.

Wi-Fi connections can be blocked or the [network throughput](/wiki/Network_throughput "Network throughput") lowered by having other devices in the same area. Wi-Fi protocols are designed to share the [radio bandwidth](/wiki/Radio_bandwidth "Radio bandwidth") reasonably fairly. To minimize collisions with Wi-Fi and non-Wi-Fi devices, Wi-Fi employs [Carrier-sense multiple access with collision avoidance](/wiki/Carrier-sense_multiple_access_with_collision_avoidance "Carrier-sense multiple access with collision avoidance") (CSMA/CA), where transmitters listen before transmitting and delay transmission of packets if they detect that other devices are active on the channel, or if noise is detected from adjacent channels or non-Wi-Fi sources. Nevertheless, Wi-Fi networks are still susceptible to the [hidden node](/wiki/Hidden_node "Hidden node") and [exposed node problems](/wiki/Exposed_node_problem "Exposed node problem").[[113]](#cite_note-119)

A standard speed Wi-Fi signal occupies five channels in the 2.4 GHz band. [Adjacent-channel interference](/wiki/Adjacent-channel_interference "Adjacent-channel interference") can be caused by overlapping channels. Any two channel numbers that differ by five or more, such as 2 and 7, do not overlap. Channels 1, 6, and 11 are the only *group of three* non-overlapping channels in North America. However, whether the overlap is significant depends on physical spacing. Channels that differ by four interfere a negligible amount – much less than reusing channels (which causes [co-channel interference](/wiki/Co-channel_interference "Co-channel interference")) – if transmitters are at least a few metres apart.[[114]](#cite_note-villegas-120) In Europe and Japan where channel 13 is available, using Channels 1, 5, 9, and 13 for [802.11g](/wiki/IEEE_802.11#802.11g "IEEE 802.11") and [802.11n](/wiki/IEEE_802.11#802.11n "IEEE 802.11") is viable and [recommended](/wiki/IEEE_802.11#Channels_and_frequencies "IEEE 802.11").

However, multiple 2.4 GHz 802.11b and 802.11g access points default to the same channel on initial startup, contributing to congestion on certain channels. Wi-Fi pollution, or an excessive number of access points in the area, can prevent access and interfere with other devices' use of other access points, as well as decreasing overall [signal-to-noise ratio](/wiki/Signal-to-noise_ratio "Signal-to-noise ratio") (SNR). These issues can become a problem in high-density areas, such as large apartment complexes or office buildings with multiple Wi-Fi access points.[[115]](#cite_note-121) It is also an issue when municipalities[[116]](#cite_note-122) or other large entities (such as universities) seek to provide large area coverage.

Other devices use the 2.4 GHz band:[[92]](#cite_note-wired-97) microwave ovens, ISM band devices, [security cameras](/wiki/Security_camera "Security camera"), Zigbee devices, Bluetooth devices, [video senders](/wiki/Video_sender "Video sender"), cordless phones, [baby monitors](/wiki/Baby_monitor "Baby monitor"),[[117]](#cite_note-123) and, in some countries, [amateur radio](/wiki/Amateur_radio "Amateur radio"), all of which can cause significant additional interference. On some 5 GHz bands interference from radar systems can occur in some places. Access points that support those bands employ [dynamic frequency selection](/wiki/Dynamic_frequency_selection "Dynamic frequency selection"), which listens for radar, and if detected, will not permit transmission on that band.

These bands can be used by low-power transmitters without a licence, and with few restrictions. However, while unintended interference is common, users who have been found to cause deliberate interference (particularly for attempting to locally monopolize these bands for commercial purposes) have been issued large fines.[[118]](#cite_note-124)

### Throughput

Various layer-2 variants of IEEE 802.11 have different characteristics. Across all flavours of 802.11, maximum achievable throughputs are either given based on measurements under ideal conditions or in the layer-2 data rates. This, however, does not apply to typical deployments in which data are transferred between two endpoints of which at least one is typically connected to a wired infrastructure, and the other is connected to an infrastructure via a wireless link.

This means that typically data frames pass an 802.11 (WLAN) medium and are being converted to 802.3 (Ethernet) or vice versa.

Due to the difference in the frame (header) lengths of these two media, the packet size of an application determines the speed of the data transfer. This means that an application that uses small packets (e.g. VoIP) creates a data flow with high overhead traffic (low [goodput](/wiki/Goodput "Goodput")).

Other factors that contribute to the overall application data rate are the speed with which the application transmits the packets (i.e. the data rate) and the energy with which the wireless signal is received. The latter is determined by distance and by the configured output power of the communicating devices.[[119]](#cite_note-125)[[120]](#cite_note-126)

## Hardware

OSBRiDGE 3GN – [802.11n](/wiki/IEEE_802.11n-2009 "IEEE 802.11n-2009") access point and [UMTS/GSM](/wiki/High-Speed_Downlink_Packet_Access "High-Speed Downlink Packet Access") gateway in one device

Wi-Fi allows wireless deployment of local area networks (LANs). Also, spaces where cables cannot be run, such as outdoor areas and historical buildings, can host wireless LANs. However, building walls of certain materials, such as stone with high metal content, can block Wi-Fi signals.

A Wi-Fi device is a [short-range](/wiki/Short-range_device "Short-range device") [wireless](/wiki/Wireless "Wireless") device. Wi-Fi devices are [fabricated](/wiki/Semiconductor_device_fabrication "Semiconductor device fabrication") on [RF CMOS](/wiki/RF_CMOS "RF CMOS") [integrated circuit](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit") ([RF circuit](/wiki/RF_circuit "RF circuit")) chips.[[121]](#cite_note-127)

Since the early 2000s, manufacturers are building wireless network adapters into most laptops. The price of [chipsets](/wiki/Chipset "Chipset") for Wi-Fi continues to drop, making it an economical networking option included in ever more devices.[[122]](#cite_note-128)

Different competitive brands of access points and client network-interfaces can inter-operate at a basic level of service. Products designated as "Wi-Fi Certified" by the Wi-Fi Alliance are [backward compatible](/wiki/Backward_compatible "Backward compatible"). Unlike [mobile phones](/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone"), any standard Wi-Fi device works anywhere in the world.

### Access point

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Apple-Airport-Extreme-80211g-WiFi-Card.jpg/250px-Apple-Airport-Extreme-80211g-WiFi-Card.jpg)](/wiki/File:Apple-Airport-Extreme-80211g-WiFi-Card.jpg)

An [AirPort](/wiki/AirPort "AirPort") Wi‑Fi adapter, supporting 802.11g, from an Apple [MacBook](/wiki/MacBook "MacBook")

A wireless access point (WAP) connects a group of wireless devices to an adjacent wired LAN. An access point resembles a [network hub](/wiki/Ethernet_hub "Ethernet hub"), relaying [data](/wiki/Data_(computing) "Data (computing)") between connected wireless devices in addition to a (usually) single connected wired device, most often an Ethernet hub or switch, allowing wireless devices to communicate with other wired devices.

### Wireless adapter

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Wireless_network_interface_controller_Gigabyte_GC-WB867D-I_-_front_and_back_-_2018-05-15.jpg/250px-Wireless_network_interface_controller_Gigabyte_GC-WB867D-I_-_front_and_back_-_2018-05-15.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wireless_network_interface_controller_Gigabyte_GC-WB867D-I_-_front_and_back_-_2018-05-15.jpg)

Wireless network interface controller Gigabyte GC-WB867D-I

Wireless adapters allow devices to connect to a wireless network. These adapters connect to devices using various external or internal interconnects such as mini PCIe ([mPCIe](/wiki/MPCIe "MPCIe"), [M.2](/wiki/M.2 "M.2")), USB, [ExpressCard](/wiki/ExpressCard "ExpressCard") and previously PCI, Cardbus, and [PC Card](/wiki/PC_Card "PC Card"). As of 2010, most newer laptop computers come equipped with built-in internal adapters.

### Router

[Wireless routers](/wiki/Wireless_router "Wireless router") integrate a Wireless Access Point, Ethernet [switch](/wiki/Network_switch "Network switch"), and internal router firmware application that provides [IP](/wiki/Internet_Protocol "Internet Protocol") [routing](/wiki/Routing "Routing"), [NAT](/wiki/Network_address_translation "Network address translation"), and [DNS](/wiki/DNS "DNS") forwarding through an integrated WAN-interface. A wireless router allows wired and wireless Ethernet LAN devices to connect to a (usually) single WAN device such as a cable modem, [DSL modem](/wiki/DSL_modem "DSL modem"), or [optical modem](/wiki/Optical_modem "Optical modem"). A wireless router allows all three devices, mainly the access point and router, to be configured through one central utility. This utility is usually an integrated [web server](/wiki/Web_server "Web server") that is accessible to wired and wireless LAN clients and often optionally to WAN clients. This utility may also be an application that is run on a computer, as is the case with as Apple's AirPort, which is managed with the [AirPort Utility](/wiki/AirPort_Utility "AirPort Utility") on [macOS](/wiki/MacOS "MacOS") and iOS.[[123]](#cite_note-129)

### Bridge

Wireless [network bridges](/wiki/Network_bridge "Network bridge") can act to connect two networks to form a single network at the [data-link layer](/wiki/OSI_model#Layer_2:_Data_link_layer "OSI model") over Wi-Fi. The main standard is the [wireless distribution system](/wiki/Wireless_distribution_system "Wireless distribution system") (WDS).

Wireless bridging can connect a wired network to a wireless network. A bridge differs from an access point: an access point typically connects wireless devices to one wired network. Two wireless bridge devices may be used to connect two wired networks over a wireless link, useful in situations where a wired connection may be unavailable, such as between two separate homes or for devices that have no wireless networking capability (but have wired networking capability), such as [consumer entertainment devices](/wiki/Consumer_electronics "Consumer electronics"); alternatively, a wireless bridge can be used to enable a device that supports a wired connection to operate at a wireless networking standard that is faster than supported by the wireless network connectivity feature (external dongle or inbuilt) supported by the device (e.g., enabling Wireless-N speeds (up to the maximum supported speed on the wired Ethernet port on both the bridge and connected devices including the wireless access point) for a device that only supports Wireless-G).
A dual-band wireless bridge can also be used to enable 5 GHz wireless network operation on a device that only supports 2.4 GHz wireless and has a wired Ethernet port.

### Repeater

Wireless range-extenders or [wireless repeaters](/wiki/Wireless_repeater "Wireless repeater") can extend the range of an existing wireless network. Strategically placed range-extenders can elongate a signal area or allow for the signal area to reach around barriers such as those pertaining in L-shaped corridors. Wireless devices connected through repeaters suffer from an increased latency for each hop, and there may be a reduction in the maximum available data throughput. Besides, the effect of additional users using a network employing wireless range-extenders is to consume the available bandwidth faster than would be the case whereby a single user migrates around a network employing extenders. For this reason, wireless range-extenders work best in networks supporting low traffic throughput requirements, such as for cases whereby a single user with a Wi-Fi-equipped tablet migrates around the combined extended and non-extended portions of the total connected network. Also, a wireless device connected to any of the repeaters in the chain has data throughput limited by the "weakest link" in the chain between the connection origin and connection end. Networks using wireless extenders are more prone to degradation from interference from neighbouring access points that border portions of the extended network and that happen to occupy the same channel as the extended network.

### Embedded systems

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Ezurio_wism2_small.jpg/250px-Ezurio_wism2_small.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ezurio_wism2_small.jpg)

Embedded serial-to-Wi-Fi module

The security standard, [Wi-Fi Protected Setup](/wiki/Wi-Fi_Protected_Setup "Wi-Fi Protected Setup"), allows embedded devices with a limited graphical user interface to connect to the Internet with ease. Wi-Fi Protected Setup has 2 configurations: The Push Button configuration and the PIN configuration. These embedded devices are also called The [Internet of things](/wiki/Internet_of_things "Internet of things") and are low-power, battery-operated embedded systems. Several Wi-Fi manufacturers design chips and modules for embedded Wi-Fi, such as GainSpan.[[124]](#cite_note-130)

Increasingly in the last few years (particularly as of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi&action=edit)), embedded Wi-Fi modules have become available that incorporate a real-time operating system and provide a simple means of wirelessly enabling any device that can communicate via a serial port.[[125]](#cite_note-131) This allows the design of simple monitoring devices. An example is a portable ECG device monitoring a patient at home. This Wi-Fi-enabled device can communicate via the Internet.[[126]](#cite_note-132)

These Wi-Fi modules are designed by [OEMs](/wiki/OEM "OEM") so that implementers need only minimal Wi-Fi knowledge to provide Wi-Fi connectivity for their products.

In June 2014, [Texas Instruments](/wiki/Texas_Instruments "Texas Instruments") introduced the first ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller with an onboard dedicated Wi-Fi MCU, the SimpleLink CC3200. It makes embedded systems with Wi-Fi connectivity possible to build as single-chip devices, which reduces their cost and minimum size, making it more practical to build wireless-networked controllers into inexpensive ordinary objects.[[127]](#cite_note-133)

## Security

Main article: [Wireless security](/wiki/Wireless_security "Wireless security")

The main issue with wireless [network security](/wiki/Network_security "Network security") is its simplified access to the network compared to traditional wired networks such as Ethernet. With wired networking, one must either gain access to a building (physically connecting into the internal network), or break through an external [firewall](/wiki/Firewall_(computing) "Firewall (computing)"). To access Wi-Fi, one must merely be within the range of the Wi-Fi network. Most business networks protect sensitive data and systems by attempting to disallow external access. Enabling wireless connectivity reduces security if the network uses inadequate or no encryption.[[128]](#cite_note-134)[[129]](#cite_note-135)[[130]](#cite_note-136)

An attacker who has gained access to a Wi-Fi network router can initiate a DNS spoofing attack against any other user of the network by forging a response before the queried DNS server has a chance to reply.[[131]](#cite_note-137)

### Securing methods

A common measure to deter unauthorized users involves hiding the access point's name by disabling the SSID broadcast. While effective against the casual user, it is ineffective as a security method because the SSID is broadcast in the clear in response to a client SSID query. Another method is to only allow computers with known MAC addresses to join the network,[[132]](#cite_note-138) but determined eavesdroppers may be able to join the network by [spoofing](/wiki/MAC_spoofing "MAC spoofing") an authorized address.

[Wired Equivalent Privacy](/wiki/Wired_Equivalent_Privacy "Wired Equivalent Privacy") (WEP) encryption was designed to protect against casual snooping but it is no longer considered secure. Tools such as [AirSnort](/w/index.php?title=AirSnort&action=edit&redlink=1 "AirSnort (page does not exist)") or [Aircrack-ng](/wiki/Aircrack-ng "Aircrack-ng") can quickly recover WEP encryption keys.[[133]](#cite_note-139) Because of WEP's weakness the Wi-Fi Alliance approved Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA) which uses [TKIP](/wiki/Temporal_Key_Integrity_Protocol "Temporal Key Integrity Protocol"). WPA was specifically designed to work with older equipment usually through a firmware upgrade. Though more secure than WEP, WPA has known vulnerabilities.

The more secure [WPA2](/wiki/WPA2 "WPA2") using [Advanced Encryption Standard](/wiki/Advanced_Encryption_Standard "Advanced Encryption Standard") was introduced in 2004 and is supported by most new Wi-Fi devices. WPA2 is fully compatible with WPA.[[134]](#cite_note-wpa2-mandatory-140) In 2017, a flaw in the WPA2 protocol was discovered, allowing a key replay attack, known as [KRACK](/wiki/KRACK "KRACK").[[135]](#cite_note-141)[[136]](#cite_note-142)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/QR_code_Wi-Fi_generic.svg/250px-QR_code_Wi-Fi_generic.svg.png)](/wiki/File:QR_code_Wi-Fi_generic.svg)

A [QR code](/wiki/QR_code "QR code") to automate a Wi-Fi connection, encoding the information *WIFI:S:Wikipedia;T:WPA;P:Password1!;;*

A flaw in a feature added to Wi-Fi in 2007, called [Wi-Fi Protected Setup](/wiki/Wi-Fi_Protected_Setup "Wi-Fi Protected Setup") (WPS), let WPA and WPA2 security be bypassed. The only remedy as of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi&action=edit) was to turn off Wi-Fi Protected Setup,[[137]](#cite_note-143) which is not always possible.

[Virtual private networks](/wiki/Virtual_private_network "Virtual private network") can be used to improve the confidentiality of data carried through Wi-Fi networks, especially public Wi-Fi networks.[[138]](#cite_note-144)

A [URI](/wiki/URI "URI") using the Wi-Fi scheme can specify the SSID, encryption type, password/passphrase, and if the SSID is hidden or not, so users can follow links from [QR codes](/wiki/QR_code "QR code"), for instance, to join networks without having to manually enter the data.[[139]](#cite_note-145) A [MeCard](/wiki/MeCard_(QR_code) "MeCard (QR code)")-like format is supported by Android and iOS 11+.[[140]](#cite_note-146)

* Common format: `WIFI:S:<SSID>;T:<WEP|WPA|blank>;P:<PASSWORD>;H:<true|false|blank>;`
* Sample `WIFI:S:MySSID;T:WPA;P:MyPassW0rd;;`

### Data security risks

Wi-Fi access points typically default to an encryption-free (*open*) mode. Novice users benefit from a zero-configuration device that works out-of-the-box, but this default does not enable any [wireless security](/wiki/Wireless_security "Wireless security"), providing open wireless access to a LAN. To turn security on requires the user to configure the device, usually via a software [graphical user interface](/wiki/Graphical_user_interface "Graphical user interface") (GUI). On unencrypted Wi-Fi networks connecting devices can monitor and record data (including personal information). Such networks can only be secured by using other means of protection, such as a [VPN](/wiki/VPN "VPN"), or [Hypertext Transfer Protocol](/wiki/Hypertext_Transfer_Protocol "Hypertext Transfer Protocol") over [Transport Layer Security](/wiki/Transport_Layer_Security "Transport Layer Security") ([HTTPS](/wiki/HTTPS "HTTPS")).

The older wireless-[encryption](/wiki/Encryption "Encryption") standard, Wired Equivalent Privacy (WEP), has been [shown](/wiki/Fluhrer,_Mantin,_and_Shamir_attack "Fluhrer, Mantin, and Shamir attack") easily breakable even when correctly configured. Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA) encryption, which became available in devices in 2003, aimed to solve this problem. Wi-Fi Protected Access 2 (WPA2) ratified in 2004 is considered secure, provided a strong [passphrase](/wiki/Passphrase "Passphrase") is used. The 2003 version of WPA has not been considered secure since it was superseded by WPA2 in 2004.

In 2018, [WPA3](/wiki/WPA3 "WPA3") was announced as a replacement for WPA2, increasing security;[[141]](#cite_note-147) it rolled out on 26 June.[[142]](#cite_note-148)

### Piggybacking

Main article: [Piggybacking (Internet access)](/wiki/Piggybacking_(Internet_access) "Piggybacking (Internet access)")

Further information: [Legality of piggybacking](/wiki/Legality_of_piggybacking "Legality of piggybacking")

Further information: [Wi-Fi Protected Setup § Physical security issues](/wiki/Wi-Fi_Protected_Setup#Physical_security_issues "Wi-Fi Protected Setup")

Piggybacking refers to access to a wireless Internet connection by bringing one's computer within the range of another's wireless connection, and using that service without the subscriber's explicit permission or knowledge.

During the early popular adoption of [802.11](/wiki/802.11 "802.11"), providing open access points for anyone within range to use was encouraged[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] to cultivate [wireless community networks](/wiki/Wireless_community_network "Wireless community network"),[[143]](#cite_note-149) particularly since people on average use only a fraction of their downstream bandwidth at any given time.

Recreational logging and mapping of other people's access points have become known as [wardriving](/wiki/Wardriving "Wardriving"). Indeed, many access points are intentionally installed without security turned on so that they can be used as a free service. Providing access to one's Internet connection in this fashion may breach the Terms of Service or contract with the [Internet service provider](/wiki/Internet_service_provider "Internet service provider") (ISP). These activities do not result in sanctions in most jurisdictions; however, legislation and [case law](/wiki/Case_law "Case law") differ considerably across the world. A proposal to leave [graffiti](/wiki/Graffiti "Graffiti") describing available services was called [warchalking](/wiki/Warchalking "Warchalking").[[144]](#cite_note-150)

Piggybacking often occurs unintentionally – a technically unfamiliar user might not change the default "unsecured" settings to their access point and operating systems can be configured to connect automatically to any available wireless network. A user who happens to start up a laptop in the vicinity of an access point may find the computer has joined the network without any visible indication. Moreover, a user intending to join one network may instead end up on another one if the latter has a stronger signal. In combination with automatic discovery of other network resources (see [DHCP](/wiki/DHCP "DHCP") and [Zeroconf](/wiki/Zeroconf "Zeroconf")) this could lead wireless users to send sensitive data to the wrong middle-man when seeking a destination (see [man-in-the-middle attack](/wiki/Man-in-the-middle_attack "Man-in-the-middle attack")). For example, a user could inadvertently use an unsecured network to log into a [website](/wiki/Website "Website"), thereby making the login credentials available to anyone listening, if the website uses an insecure protocol such as plain [HTTP](/wiki/HTTP "HTTP") without [TLS](/wiki/Transport_Layer_Security "Transport Layer Security").

On an unsecured access point, an unauthorized user can obtain security information (factory preset passphrase or Wi-Fi Protected Setup PIN) from a label on a wireless access point and use this information (or connect by the Wi-Fi Protected Setup pushbutton method) to commit unauthorized or unlawful activities.

## Societal aspects

See also: [Internet § Social Impact](/wiki/Internet#Social_Impact "Internet")

Wireless Internet access has become much more embedded in society. It has thus changed how the society functions in a number of ways.

### Influence on developing countries

See also: [Long-range Wi-Fi](/wiki/Long-range_Wi-Fi "Long-range Wi-Fi")

As of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi&action=edit) over half the world did not have access to the Internet,[[77]](#cite_note-:1-79) prominently rural areas in developing nations. Technology that has been implemented in more developed nations is often costly and energy inefficient. This has led to developing nations using more low-tech networks, frequently implementing renewable power sources that can solely be maintained through [solar power](/wiki/Solar_power "Solar power"), creating a network that is resistant to disruptions such as power outages. For instance, in 2007, a 450-kilometre (280 mi) network between Cabo Pantoja and [Iquitos](/wiki/Iquitos "Iquitos") in [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru") was erected in which all equipment is powered only by [solar panels](/wiki/Solar_panel "Solar panel").[[77]](#cite_note-:1-79) These long-range Wi-Fi networks have two main uses: offer Internet access to populations in isolated villages, and to provide healthcare to isolated communities. In the case of the latter example, it connects the central hospital in Iquitos to 15 medical outposts which are intended for remote diagnosis.[[77]](#cite_note-:1-79)

### Work habits

Access to Wi-Fi in public spaces such as cafés or parks allows people, in particular freelancers, to work remotely. While the accessibility of Wi-Fi is the strongest factor when choosing a place to work (75% of people would choose a place that provides Wi-Fi over one that does not),[[72]](#cite_note-:2-74) other factors influence the choice of specific [hotspots](/wiki/Wi-Fi_hotspot "Wi-Fi hotspot"). These vary from the accessibility of other resources, like books, the location of the workplace, and the social aspect of meeting other people in the same place. Moreover, the increase of people working from public places results in more customers for local businesses thus providing an economic stimulus to the area.

Additionally, in the same study it has been noted that wireless connection provides more freedom of movement while working. Both when working at home or from the office it allows the displacement between different rooms or areas. In some offices (notably Cisco offices in New York) the employees do not have assigned desks but can work from any office connecting their laptop to Wi-Fi [hotspot](/wiki/Hotspot_(Wi-Fi) "Hotspot (Wi-Fi)").[[72]](#cite_note-:2-74)

### Housing

The Internet has become an integral part of living. As of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi&action=edit), 81.9% of American households have Internet access.[[145]](#cite_note-151) Additionally, 89% of American households with broadband connect via wireless technologies.[[146]](#cite_note-152) 72.9% of American households have Wi-Fi.

Wi-Fi networks have also affected how the interior of homes and hotels are arranged. For instance, architects have described that their clients no longer wanted only one room as their home office, but would like to work near the fireplace or have the possibility to work in different rooms. This contradicts architect's pre-existing ideas of the use of rooms that they designed. Additionally, some hotels have noted that guests prefer to stay in certain rooms since they receive a stronger Wi-Fi signal.[[72]](#cite_note-:2-74)

## Health concerns

Further information: [Wireless device radiation and health](/wiki/Wireless_device_radiation_and_health "Wireless device radiation and health")

The [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") (WHO) says, "no health effects are expected from exposure to RF fields from base stations and wireless networks", but notes that they promote research into effects from other RF sources.[[147]](#cite_note-153) Although the WHO's [International Agency for Research on Cancer](/wiki/International_Agency_for_Research_on_Cancer "International Agency for Research on Cancer") (IARC) later classified radio-frequency [electromagnetic fields](/wiki/Electromagnetic_field "Electromagnetic field") (EMFs) as "possibly carcinogenic to humans ([Group 2B](/wiki/List_of_IARC_Group_2B_carcinogens "List of IARC Group 2B carcinogens"))"[[148]](#cite_note-154) (a category used when "a causal association is considered credible, but when chance, bias or confounding cannot be ruled out with reasonable confidence"),[[149]](#cite_note-155) this classification was based on risks associated with wireless phone use rather than Wi-Fi networks.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The United Kingdom's [Health Protection Agency](/wiki/Health_Protection_Agency "Health Protection Agency") reported in 2007 that exposure to Wi-Fi for a year results in the "same amount of radiation from a 20-minute mobile phone call".[[150]](#cite_note-156)

A review of studies involving 725 people who claimed [electromagnetic hypersensitivity](/wiki/Electromagnetic_hypersensitivity "Electromagnetic hypersensitivity"), "...suggests that 'electromagnetic hypersensitivity' is unrelated to the presence of an EMF, although more research into this phenomenon is required."[[151]](#cite_note-157)

## Alternatives

Several other wireless technologies provide alternatives to Wi-Fi for different use cases:

* [Bluetooth Low Energy](/wiki/Bluetooth_Low_Energy "Bluetooth Low Energy"), a low-power variant of Bluetooth
* [Bluetooth](/wiki/Bluetooth "Bluetooth"), a short-distance network
* [Cellular networks](/wiki/Cellular_network "Cellular network"), used by smartphones
* [LoRa](/wiki/LoRa "LoRa"), for long range wireless with low data rate
* [NearLink](/wiki/NearLink "NearLink"), a short-range wireless technology standard
* [WiMAX](/wiki/WiMAX "WiMAX"), for providing long range wireless internet connectivity
* [Zigbee](/wiki/Zigbee "Zigbee"), a low-power, low data rate, short-distance communication protocol

Some alternatives are "no new wires", re-using existing cable:

* [G.hn](/wiki/G.hn "G.hn"), which uses existing home wiring, such as phone and [power lines](/wiki/Power_line_communication "Power line communication")

Several *wired* technologies for computer networking, which provide viable alternatives to Wi-Fi:

* [Ethernet over twisted pair](/wiki/Ethernet_over_twisted_pair "Ethernet over twisted pair")
